summary: This is an advice column where Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') answers reader questions on topics ranging from crepe recipes to dating advice to the mathematical foundations needed for data science. The most substantive response addresses whether someone needs innate math talent to become a data scientist, where she argues that understanding concepts like matrix inversion isn't about effortless genius but about developing the detective skills to debug models when they produce garbage. She emphasizes that data science requires understanding computational fragility—like what happens when you try to invert a nearly-singular matrix—because models never work correctly on the first attempt. She also briefly dismisses a misogynistic article about women in tech and provides thoughtful dating advice focused on self-improvement and putting oneself out there.
key_points: Data scientists don't need to memorize matrix inversion formulas, but must understand computational fragility when dealing with nearly-singular matrices
A data scientist's job is mostly figuring out why their model is 'fucking up massively'—models never work the first, second, or 17th time
Real-world data is always an approximation of truth, which creates computational problems that manifest as garbage outputs rather than clear error messages
Mathematical aptitude for data science isn't about instant effortless understanding—it's about developing detective skills to backtrack from wrong answers to root causes
The word 'shrill' in an article is an immediate tell that the author is a woman hater
money_quotes: The data scientist's job is mostly to figure out why their model is fucking up massively.
Models never work the first, second, or 17th time they are run, so you'd better be good at understanding what's going wrong.
When you're dealing with real world data, everything is an approximation of truth.
None of this stuff is easy or effortless, so throw away that misconception immediately.
You're not supposed to 'invert' an uninvertible matrix, but you of course can invert a matrix that has incredibly small but non-zero determinant. What you end up with is garbage.
Good news is he gave himself away early with the word 'shrill'; after that I knew he was a woman hater.
About half of self-confidence is the belief that other people will want to spend time with you.
key_insight: The real skill in data science isn't mathematical brilliance but the detective work of understanding why computations produce garbage—and the conceptual knowledge to trace problems back to their mathematical roots.
